content.text: Server-Sent Events (SSE) is a standardised HTTP-based protocol that enables servers to push a stream of text events to clients over a single persistent connection. Unlike WebSockets, SSE is unidirectional — only the server sends data — which makes it simpler to implement, proxy-friendly, and natively supported by all modern browsers through the EventSource API.

Server-Sent Events is defined in the WHATWG HTML Living Standard. A client opens an HTTP connection with the Accept: text/event-stream header. The server responds with Content-Type: text/event-stream and keeps the connection open, writing line-delimited text frames — events — as they become available. Each event consists of one or more field lines followed by a blank line.

The four fields a server can emit are: data (the payload, required), event (an optional event type name), id (a cursor for reconnection), and retry (a millisecond reconnect delay instruction). A blank line terminates and dispatches an event to the client's onmessage or named-event listeners.

SSE is the protocol underlying the streaming responses from the OpenAI Chat Completions API, Anthropic Messages API, and most modern LLM providers. Each token generated by the model is sent as a data: field as soon as it is produced, allowing the client to begin rendering text before the full response is complete.

Users strongly prefer seeing text appear incrementally over waiting for a full response to arrive at once. Studies on perceived performance consistently show that time-to-first-byte is more important than total response time for perceived responsiveness — an LLM completing 200 tokens in 4 seconds with immediate first-token delivery feels faster than one that delivers all 200 tokens simultaneously after 3 seconds.

SSE enables this experience with minimal infrastructure complexity. Unlike WebSockets, SSE connections can be proxied by standard HTTP infrastructure (nginx, CDNs, load balancers), require no protocol upgrade, and work over HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2 without modification. The browser's EventSource automatically handles reconnection, removing failure-mode complexity from the application developer.

In agentic AI systems, SSE enables real-time feedback from long-running processes: streaming intermediate reasoning steps, progress indicators during multi-tool agent loops, and partial document generation. These patterns transform the user experience from 'waiting for AI' to 'watching AI think'.
No WebSocket handshake; works with existing HTTP infrastructure, proxies, and load balancers out of the box.
The browser's EventSource automatically reconnects and sends Last-Event-ID, enabling resumable streams.
Multiple logical event types (message, progress, error, done) can be multiplexed over a single connection.
Plain text framing; no binary encoding; extremely easy to parse and debug in browser DevTools.
Over HTTP/2, multiple SSE streams share one TCP connection, eliminating the browser's 6-connection-per-host limit.
Aligns perfectly with the token-by-token generation pattern of auto-regressive language models.
The client cannot send messages over the SSE connection. For bidirectional communication, WebSockets or a separate POST endpoint is needed.
Browsers limit concurrent HTTP/1.1 connections to 6 per host; multiple SSE streams on HTTP/1.1 consume this budget.
The SSE format is text-only; binary payloads must be base64-encoded, adding ~33% overhead.
Misconfigured proxies can buffer SSE responses, preventing incremental delivery. Proper headers (X-Accel-Buffering: no) must be set.
The server cannot confirm that the client received each event; delivery confirmation requires the client to separately ACK via HTTP.
Each SSE event is a block of field-value lines terminated by a blank line (two consecutive newlines). The field name and value are separated by a colon and optional space.

Multi-line data is expressed by repeating the data: field; the browser concatenates values with newline characters between them. The id field sets the Last-Event-ID state, which is resent on reconnection via the Last-Event-ID request header.

The browser opens an EventSource, which immediately issues a GET request with Accept: text/event-stream. If the connection was previously open, Last-Event-ID is included. The server returns 200 OK with Content-Type: text/event-stream and Cache-Control: no-cache, then streams events indefinitely. If the connection drops, the browser waits for the retry interval (default 3 seconds, overridable via the retry: field) and reconnects automatically.

LLM provider APIs use SSE with a single data: field per token or chunk. The final event in the stream is data: [DONE], which is a sentinel value the client uses to close the EventSource and stop processing. This pattern is not part of the SSE specification — it is a convention adopted by OpenAI and followed by compatible providers.

attrs.alt: SSE protocol flow: HTTP connection, event stream, named events, IDs, retry and reconnection
SSE protocol flow showing client connection, event stream, and auto-reconnect

content.text: The server generates data incrementally and the client should display it as it arrives (LLM token streaming, live logs, progress updates).
The communication is server-to-client only — no client-to-server messages over the stream.
You need to work within standard HTTP infrastructure without WebSocket support.
Automatic reconnection and resume are important for reliability.
You want easy debugging — SSE events are visible in browser Network tab.
The client needs to send frequent messages over the same connection (chat with typing indicators, multiplayer games, collaborative editing).
Binary data transfer is required without base64 overhead.
The application needs full-duplex streaming in both directions simultaneously.

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
import asyncio, json

app = FastAPI()

async def token_stream(prompt: str):
    """Simulate LLM token streaming with SSE events."""
    tokens = ["The", " quick", " brown", " fox", " jumps", "."]
    for i, token in enumerate(tokens):
        data = json.dumps({"token": token, "index": i})
        yield f"id: {i}\ndata: {data}\n\n"
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # simulate model generation latency
    yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"

@app.get("/stream")
async def stream_endpoint(prompt: str = "Hello"):
    return StreamingResponse(
        token_stream(prompt),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
            "Connection": "keep-alive",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",  # Disable nginx buffering
        },
    )
Example 1 — OpenAI streaming with Python requests
Example 2 — Full streaming chat with resume support

import asyncio, json, aiohttp
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

client = AsyncOpenAI()

async def streaming_chat(
    messages: list[dict],
    on_token=None,
    on_complete=None,
) -> str:
    """
    Stream a chat completion, calling on_token for each token
    and on_complete with the full text when done.
    """
    full_text = ""
    async with client.chat.completions.stream(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=messages,
    ) as stream:
        async for chunk in stream:
            token = chunk.choices[0].delta.content or ""
            if token:
                full_text += token
                if on_token:
                    await on_token(token)
    if on_complete:
        await on_complete(full_text)
    return full_text

async def main():
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Write a haiku about streaming data."},
    ]
    tokens_received = []
    async def handle_token(t): tokens_received.append(t); print(t, end="", flush=True)
    async def handle_complete(text): print(f"\n\nTotal tokens: {len(tokens_received)}")
    await streaming_chat(messages, on_token=handle_token, on_complete=handle_complete)

asyncio.run(main())
Example 3 — SSE with named events and progress tracking

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from openai import AsyncOpenAI
import asyncio, json

app = FastAPI()
client = AsyncOpenAI()

async def agent_stream(task: str):
    """Stream an agent workflow with named SSE events."""
    # Phase 1: progress event
    yield f"event: progress\ndata: {json.dumps({'stage': 'planning', 'pct': 10})}\n\n"
    await asyncio.sleep(0.5)

    # Phase 2: stream LLM tokens as message events
    async with client.chat.completions.stream(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task}],
    ) as stream:
        token_count = 0
        async for chunk in stream:
            token = chunk.choices[0].delta.content or ""
            if token:
                token_count += 1
                yield f"data: {json.dumps({'token': token})}\n\n"
        yield f"event: progress\ndata: {json.dumps({'stage': 'done', 'pct': 100, 'tokens': token_count})}\n\n"

    yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"

@app.get("/agent")
async def agent_endpoint(task: str = "Analyse AI trends"):
    return StreamingResponse(
        agent_stream(task),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={"Cache-Control": "no-cache", "X-Accel-Buffering": "no"},
    )

A legal document analysis platform streams multi-step analysis results to the browser. The backend agent pipeline involves three stages: document parsing, legal entity extraction, and summary generation. Each stage emits named SSE events that update different parts of the UI simultaneously — a progress bar, an entity list panel, and a streaming summary text area.

from fastapi import FastAPI, UploadFile
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from openai import AsyncOpenAI
import json, asyncio

app = FastAPI()
client = AsyncOpenAI()

async def legal_analysis_stream(document_text: str):
    """Multi-stage legal analysis with named SSE events."""
    # Stage 1: Extract entities
    yield f"event: stage\ndata: {json.dumps({'name': 'entity_extraction', 'pct': 5})}\n\n"
    entity_resp = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Extract parties, dates, and obligations from this legal document. Return JSON."},
            {"role": "user", "content": document_text[:4000]},
        ],
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
        temperature=0,
    )
    entities = json.loads(entity_resp.choices[0].message.content)
    yield f"event: entities\ndata: {json.dumps(entities)}\n\n"
    yield f"event: stage\ndata: {json.dumps({'name': 'summary', 'pct': 40})}\n\n"

    # Stage 2: Stream summary
    async with client.chat.completions.stream(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Summarise the key legal obligations in plain English."},
            {"role": "user", "content": document_text[:4000]},
        ],
    ) as stream:
        async for chunk in stream:
            token = chunk.choices[0].delta.content or ""
            if token:
                yield f"data: {json.dumps({'token': token})}\n\n"

    yield f"event: stage\ndata: {json.dumps({'name': 'complete', 'pct': 100})}\n\n"
    yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"

@app.post("/analyse")
async def analyse(file: UploadFile):
    text = (await file.read()).decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
    return StreamingResponse(
        legal_analysis_stream(text),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={"Cache-Control": "no-cache", "X-Accel-Buffering": "no"},
    )
Mistake 1 — Missing the blank line between events

# WRONG: events run together, browser sees one big event
yield f"data: token1\n"
yield f"data: token2\n"

# RIGHT: each event MUST end with a blank line (double newline)
yield f"data: token1\n\n"
yield f"data: token2\n\n"

Nginx and many reverse proxies buffer responses by default. Without the X-Accel-Buffering: no header (or proxy_buffering off in nginx config), the entire SSE stream may be held in the proxy buffer until the connection closes, defeating the purpose of streaming.
Mistake 4 — Using SSE for bidirectional chat

SSE is strictly server-to-client. When the user sends a follow-up message, a new HTTP POST is required. Pairing SSE streaming with a REST POST endpoint for user messages is the correct architecture — not upgrading to WebSockets just because the interaction has both directions.
Always set Content-Type: text/event-stream and Cache-Control: no-cache on the server response.
Set X-Accel-Buffering: no to prevent nginx / CDN buffering.
Assign event IDs and handle Last-Event-ID on the server to support client reconnection without missing events.
Emit a data: [DONE]\n\n sentinel to signal stream completion, and close the EventSource client-side upon receiving it.
Use named events (event: progress) to multiplex different data streams over a single SSE connection.
Implement server-side connection cleanup: detect when the client disconnects and cancel the underlying generation task to avoid wasting compute.
For LLM streaming in Python, use the async generator pattern with FastAPI StreamingResponse to avoid blocking the event loop.
Test streaming behavior behind your production reverse proxy early — proxy buffering is the most common deployment issue.

Each SSE connection holds an open file descriptor on the server. At scale, thousands of concurrent SSE connections require tuning of OS-level limits (ulimit -n) and HTTP server worker concurrency. Use async frameworks (FastAPI/uvicorn, Node.js, Go) rather than thread-per-connection servers for SSE at scale — blocking a thread per connection is expensive.

HTTP/2 provides significant SSE scalability improvements: all SSE streams from one origin share a single TCP connection, eliminating the browser's per-host 6-connection limit and reducing TLS overhead. Server-push (a distinct HTTP/2 feature) is not the same as SSE and should not be confused with it.

For very high-throughput applications, consider a dedicated streaming gateway (e.g., the OpenAI proxy pattern, or a gateway layer using nginx stream module) that handles the SSE fan-out, while the backend LLM API response is pulled once and distributed to multiple clients when the same response is needed by multiple users.

The browser's EventSource does not support custom headers, which means bearer tokens cannot be sent in the Authorization header. Common workarounds: pass the token as a query parameter (risk: leakage in server logs), use a short-lived signed URL, or require a session cookie. For fetch-based SSE clients (not native EventSource), custom headers work normally.

EventSource is subject to CORS. The server must respond with the appropriate Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. Wildcard (*) is acceptable for public streams; credentials (cookies) require explicit origin allowlisting and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true.

A client that never closes an SSE connection holds a server file descriptor and an open LLM generation process indefinitely. Implement server-side timeout logic: if no bytes have been written to the client for N seconds (or if the LLM generation completes), forcibly close the connection and cancel any running generation.

If any part of the event data comes from user input, ensure newlines are stripped or escaped. A user-supplied string containing 

event: malicious
data: payload

 would inject a forged SSE event into the stream. Always validate and sanitise user-controlled data before embedding in SSE fields.

Think of SSE as a newspaper printing press that never stops. Once the press starts, it prints one line at a time and hands each strip to a waiting delivery person (the browser connection). The delivery person takes the strip to the reader (the EventSource listener) immediately, without waiting for the full newspaper to be printed.

The retry field is the instructions taped to the delivery person's door: 'If you lose contact with the press, wait 3 seconds and come back, telling them where you left off (Last-Event-ID).' The press knows to resume from that point, so nothing is lost.

The data: [DONE] sentinel is the press operator shouting 'That's all for today!' The delivery person stops waiting and goes home. This is not part of the official SSE protocol — it is a convention — but it is universally adopted for LLM streaming to signal stream completion unambiguously.
SSE is a standardised HTTP protocol for server-to-client streaming over a persistent connection, using plain text fields separated by blank lines.
The four SSE fields are: data (payload), event (type name), id (cursor), and retry (reconnect delay).
The browser's EventSource automatically reconnects and sends Last-Event-ID, enabling resumable streams with no application code.
data: [DONE]\n\n is the conventional stream-end sentinel used by LLM providers; it is not part of the SSE specification.
Always set X-Accel-Buffering: no and Cache-Control: no-cache to prevent reverse proxy and browser caching from buffering the stream.
SSE is unidirectional; client-to-server messages require a separate HTTP request.
Use async frameworks (FastAPI, Node.js, Go) for SSE servers — blocking threads per connection is not scalable.
Sanitise any user-controlled data in event fields to prevent newline injection of forged SSE events.
